- [cross.journeys.85] | Full feature-cycle journey: a delivered build (frozen baseline, corpus done, clean tree) → owner opens a spec cycle → Vivi grills and writes canonical (`spec_frozen` flips false) → extraction re-freezes with a minor bump (approval_ref = cycle id, prior superseded) which CLOSES the cycle → the build runs on the evolved baseline. | Each leg's state transitions as specified. | e2e-process | dev-rehearsal.ts feature-cycle stage covers open→evolve→refreeze-closes; the Vivi-authored edit + the subsequent supervised build are GAP end-to-end
- [cross.journeys.86] | Build-gated-by-open-cycle journey: with a cycle open, both the UI Run and `vivicy start` refuse with `cycle_state` and the actionable "extract to freeze it (or cancel the cycle)" message; the user must extract or cancel first. | Refusal surfaced in both surfaces. | e2e-ui | GAP (unit/CLI covered by control.test.ts / cli.test.ts; no UI cross-journey drives it)
- [cross.journeys.87] | Honest-refusal journey: `cycle.open` refused mid-build (`already_running`) and `cycle.cancel` refused after the canonical drifts (`cycle_state`), each relayed as an honest tool/notification result. | Refusals relayed truthfully. | e2e-ui | GAP

## Cross: cross-chaos-parallel-merge

Combinator over parallelism scale x dependency/claim graphs x merge-conflict/resolver outcomes x post-merge gate x crash/leftover x lock contention x done-move race, grounded in factory/dev-loop.ts (runLoop, runLoopParallel, selectIndependentBatch, issuesIndependent, withIntegrationLock, defaultCreateWorktree, defaultIntegrateWorktree, defaultResetWorktreeFrozenArtifacts, defaultResetHard, defaultRebaseWorktree, commitDoneMove, moveIssueToDone), factory/dev-loop-supervised.ts (nextSupervisorAction, main relaunch loop), lib/control.ts (startSupervisor/stopSupervisor/isRunActive/claimRunLock), factory/dev-loop.test.ts (existing coverage), lib/settings.ts (clampMaxParallel/MIN_PARALLEL/MAX_PARALLEL).

All ids: `[cross.chaos-parallel-merge.<n>]`.

### 1 task sequential (maxParallel = 1 / clamp floor)

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.1] | `runLoop` with `cfg.maxParallel` unset/1 drives issues one at a time against the main root, no worktree ever created. | Identical to today's sequential behavior; `runLoopParallel` is never invoked (`clampConcurrency(cfg.maxParallel) > 1` is false). | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:2156 "runLoop(maxParallel=1) is the sequential path: returns an array, identical behavior".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.2] | `VIVICY_MAX_PARALLEL=0` or unparseable string reaches `clampConcurrency` and floors to 1 (sequential), never spawns a worktree scheduler at 0 concurrency. | `clampConcurrency(0) === 1`; loop takes the sync path. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:1350 "clampConcurrency floors bad values to 1".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.3] | UI Settings dialog sets maxParallel via `lib/settings.ts` `clampMaxParallel`, then `settingsToEnv` stamps `VIVICY_MAX_PARALLEL` into the supervisor's spawn env (lib/control.ts `startSupervisor` -> `supervisorEnv`). A value of `1` from Settings round-trips to sequential dev-loop behavior end-to-end (UI -> env -> DEFAULT_CONFIG.maxParallel -> clampConcurrency -> sync path). | The whole vertical slice (Settings dialog -> spawned supervisor env -> dev-loop concurrency mode) is never exercised together. | e2e-process | GAP.
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.4] | Exactly 1 ready issue exists (no batch to spread) even though `maxParallel` is configured > 1. | `selectIndependentBatch` seeds with the single ready issue and the while-loop finds no further eligible candidate (`best` stays null), returning a 1-issue batch; `runLoopParallel` still creates ONE worktree, integrates it, and proceeds — parallel machinery degrades to a single concurrent worker. | unit | GAP (selectIndependentBatch is tested with multi-issue ready sets in dev-loop.test.ts:1408 "limit 1 returns exactly one ready issue" but that test uses maxParallel semantics on the SELECTION function, not a full `runLoopParallel` run with maxParallel=3 and only 1 ready issue).

### All tasks parallel (batch == full ready set, all mutually independent)

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.5] | All N issues in the index are mutually independent (disjoint claims, no dependency edges) and `maxParallel >= N`. | `computeReadySet` returns all N; `selectIndependentBatch` admits every one (each pairwise-independent check passes); N worktrees are created, N issues run+integrate concurrently, `running.size` drains to 0 in one wave. | integration | GAP (existing "runLoopParallel runs independent issues concurrently" test at dev-loop.test.ts:1756 uses a small fixed set (2-3 issues), not an "all issues, full width" scenario verifying every issue got its own worktree and none were excluded by the spread heuristic).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.6] | All issues are independent but architecture-data.json is present with real cluster/adjacency data: max-spread selection (`selectIndependentBatch` farthest-point sampling) must still admit every mutually-independent issue when `limit >= ready.length`, not stop early because spread scoring found "no further eligible candidate" prematurely. | Batch size equals `min(limit, ready.length)`; the loop in `selectIndependentBatch` (`while (batch.length < slots)`) only breaks when `!best`, which only happens when every remaining ready issue fails the hard independence gate — with all issues genuinely independent, every one is eventually admitted. | unit | GAP (spread tests exercise distinctness of clusters, not that a "run everything in parallel" batch loses no eligible issue).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.7] | `maxParallel` is configured but the ready set momentarily EXCEEDS it (e.g. 20 ready issues, maxParallel=12): the scheduler must select at most `maxParallel` and leave the rest for the next re-schedule wave once a slot frees. | `selectIndependentBatch`'s `slots = Math.max(1, limit) - runningIssues.length` bounds the batch; on `running.size > 0` the scheduler `await`s `Promise.race(running.values())` and re-derives `ready`/`batch` next iteration, eventually draining all 20 across multiple waves. | integration | GAP.

### Exactly 12 (MAX_CONCURRENCY ceiling)

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.8] | `VIVICY_MAX_PARALLEL=12` (or any value >= 12, e.g. 999, "abc999") is clamped to exactly `MAX_CONCURRENCY = 12`; a run with >=12 independent ready issues schedules exactly 12 concurrent worktrees, never 13+. | `clampConcurrency` caps at 12 both in factory/dev-loop.ts and lib/settings.ts (`MAX_PARALLEL = 12`), defense-in-depth on both the UI and factory sides. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:1447 "clampConcurrency caps at MAX_CONCURRENCY = 12 (range 1..12)" (the pure clamp only, not an actual 12-worktree run).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.9] | A live `runLoopParallel` run with `maxParallel=12` and >=12 disjoint-claim ready issues creates 12 concurrent git worktrees under `.vivicy-worktrees/`, each on its own `vivicy/<id>` branch, and the 13th+ issues stay in `computeReadySet` but excluded from `running`/`runningIssueById` until a slot frees. | Exactly 12 `.git/worktrees` entries exist at peak; the 13th only gets a worktree after one of the 12 settles (verified/blocked) and its worktree is removed. | e2e-process | GAP (no test drives a real 12-way worktree fan-out; all existing parallel tests use 2-4 issues with fake worktree steps).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.10] | At 12-way concurrency, the O_EXCL integration-lock file (`<gatesDir>/.integration.lock`) is contended by up to 12 simultaneous `withIntegrationLock` callers (worktree creation + merge/done-move), and the in-process async mutex (`integrationMutexes`) serializes them without deadlock or starvation. | All 12 tasks eventually acquire and release the lock in some order; none starves permanently (`acquireExclusiveLock`'s 30s deadline is never hit under normal completion times); no two `git merge`/`git worktree add` calls interleave on the main root. | integration | GAP (existing "ledger consistency under many concurrent completions" test at dev-loop.test.ts:1897 checks ledger event-count integrity, not lock-acquisition fairness/ordering at N=12, nor the file-lock path specifically — most parallel tests bypass real file-lock timing via fake worktree/gate steps).

### Batch selection with dependency graphs (transitive deps, disjoint-claims violations)

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.11] | Issue C depends on B which depends on A (a 2-hop chain); A is done, B and C both appear "ready" by direct-dependency check alone, but C transitively depends on A only through B — `issuesIndependent(B, C, depsClosureById)` must refuse to co-schedule B and C even though only B is C's DIRECT dependency. | `buildDepsClosure` computes the full transitive closure so `bDeps.has(a.id) || aDeps.has(b.id)` catches multi-hop chains; B and C are never selected into the same batch. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:1379 "buildDepsClosure resolves transitive dependencies" + :1583 "max-spread NEVER co-schedules dependent issues".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.12] | Two ready issues D and E declare disjoint `graph_refs` but the SAME `claimed_files` entry (an issue claims files explicitly, overriding graph_refs) — the disjoint-claims violation must be caught via `issueClaim`'s explicit-claims-first precedence, not the (disjoint) graph_refs. | `issuesIndependent` returns false for (D, E) because `issueClaim` returns the `claimed_files` set (not graph_refs) and `setsIntersect` finds the shared file; D and E are never batched together. | unit | GAP (existing "issueClaim prefers explicit claims" test at :1373 and "issuesIndependent" test at :1391 exist separately, but no test combines "disjoint graph_refs + overlapping claimed_files" as the specific trap this precedence rule guards against).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.13] | A cyclic `depends_on` graph (issue X depends on Y, Y depends on X — malformed/corrupt issue index) is fed to `buildDepsClosure`. | The cycle guard (`if (stack.has(id)) return new Set()`) prevents infinite recursion; closure resolves to an empty (or partial) set for the cyclic pair rather than a stack overflow, and the scheduler does not crash (though it may under- or over-schedule the cyclic pair — a degraded-but-safe outcome). | unit | GAP (no test constructs a cyclic depends_on graph; only the "should never happen" comment documents the guard).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.14] | A batch candidate is independent of every issue already in `batch` but NOT independent of a currently-`running` issue (from a prior wave still in flight). | `selectIndependentBatch`'s `eligible()` check requires independence against BOTH `runningIssues` and `batch`; the candidate is excluded even though it would look independent using batch-only criteria. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:1431 "selectIndependentBatch respects already-running issues (no shared claim with them)".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.15] | Empty ready set (0 issues ready — everything done, blocked, or transitively gated) with `maxParallel > 1`: `selectIndependentBatch` must return `[]` without throwing (the `ready.find(...)` seed search returns undefined). | `if (!seed) return batch;` returns an empty array cleanly; the scheduler's `running.size === 0` check then terminates the run. | unit | GAP (no direct test calls `selectIndependentBatch` with an empty `ready` array; only indirectly exercised inside larger runLoopParallel fixtures with a resolved final state).

### Two issues editing the same file -> conflict -> merge-resolver -> retry -> block

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.16] | Two INDEPENDENT-per-claims issues (disjoint declared `graph_refs`) each have their implementer agent ACTUALLY edit the same real source file (an out-of-band file touch the claims system didn't anticipate) — their worktree branches diverge on that file and `defaultIntegrateWorktree`'s merge conflicts. | `merge.ok === false`; `git merge --abort` runs immediately so main is left untouched; the conflict is surfaced as `conflict: true` with the git stderr/stdout message. | integration | GAP (no test simulates a REAL conflicting file edit across two worktrees at the git level with the default git-backed steps — all "conflict" tests inject a fake `integrateWorktree` step returning `{ok:false, conflict:true}` directly, e.g. dev-loop.test.ts:2643 onward).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.17] | On merge conflict, the resolver path runs exactly once: `wt.rebaseWorktree` rebases the worktree branch onto the current integration HEAD, THEN a bounded merge-resolver leg (implementer CLI, role `merge-resolver`) runs in the worktree, writes a `<id>-merge-resolution.json` verdict, the orchestrator re-runs the gate itself in the worktree (`runGateAt(issueCfg)`), and ONLY IF `verdict.resolved === true && worktreeGate.pass` does it retry the merge exactly once. | Merge succeeds on retry; `merge_conflict_resolved` is emitted with role `merge-resolver`; frozen-artifact edits are re-neutralized (`wt.resetFrozenArtifacts` called a second time) before the retry merge. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:2643 "merge conflict -> resolver resolved + worktree gate green -> merge retried once -> success".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.18] | The merge-resolver leg CLAIMS `resolved: true` in its verdict file, but the orchestrator's own worktree-gate re-run comes back RED (the resolver's self-report is not trusted). | The retry merge is never attempted (`if (verdict?.resolved === true && worktreeGate.pass)` fails on the gate half); the issue is blocked with an "integration conflict (unresolved)" reason; `merge_conflict_unresolved` is emitted. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:2691 "the resolver CLAIMS resolved but the orchestrator's worktree gate re-run is RED -> blocked (trust nothing)".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.19] | The merge-resolver verdict file is missing or malformed (leg died, or wrote invalid JSON / non-boolean `resolved`). | `readMergeResolutionVerdict` returns null; `verdict?.resolved === true` is false (optional chaining on null), so the merge is never retried and the issue blocks with the conflict as unresolved — same fail-closed path as an explicit `resolved:false`. | unit | GAP (existing tests cover `resolved:true`+red-gate and `resolved:true`+green-gate; no test exercises a MISSING/malformed resolution file specifically, though the code path (`readMergeResolutionVerdict` returning null) is the same one that backs "readiness leg death" pattern at :2436 — the merge-resolver analogue is untested).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.20] | `steps.runMergeResolver === false` (a test/operator explicitly disables the resolver path). | On a merge conflict, the resolver block is skipped entirely (`if (steps.runMergeResolver !== false)` guard) and the issue blocks immediately on the first conflict, without ever rebasing or running a resolver leg. | unit | GAP (the `!== false` escape hatch is read in the code but no test sets `runMergeResolver: false` and asserts immediate block-without-resolver-attempt).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.21] | Merge conflict resolution succeeds (retry merge is `merge.ok`), but then the SEPARATE post-merge re-gate (on the integration tree) comes back red — i.e. resolution + retry-merge succeeding does not exempt the issue from the post-merge re-gate check. | The post-merge re-gate still runs unconditionally after ANY successful merge (resolved-and-retried or clean first-try); a red post-merge gate reverts via `resetHard(preMergeSha)` and blocks the issue with `post_merge_gate` kind, exactly as a non-conflicted merge would. | integration | GAP (no test chains "conflict -> resolved -> merged" into a subsequent "post-merge gate red" — the two code paths are each tested in isolation, not composed).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.22] | The resolver leg itself is quota-blocked (rate-limited past the hard cap) while resolving a conflict. | `runLegWithQuotaAsync` wrapping the resolver leg returns without throwing (never inspected for `quotaBlocked` in the merge-resolver call site — the code discards the leg result: `await runLegWithQuotaAsync(...)` with no return-value check), so a quota-exhausted resolver silently falls through to `readMergeResolutionVerdict` returning null (no verdict file was written) -> treated as unresolved -> blocked. Confirm this is the intended behavior rather than a hang. | unit | GAP (the merge-resolver call site's quota-block return value is never asserted; the sequential/readiness quota-block paths ARE tested (`quotaBlock` cycle result), but the merge-resolver's discarded-quota-result path is not).

### Post-merge gate red -> revert

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.23] | A worktree's gate is green pre-merge, the merge itself succeeds cleanly (no conflict), but re-running the SAME gate on the resulting integration tree comes back red (the merge combined two individually-green diffs into a broken whole). | `postMergeGate.pass === false` triggers `wt.resetHard(preMergeSha)` (hard-reset main back to the pre-merge HEAD, since the merge commit is the only new commit), writes `<id>-integration-blocked.json` embedding BOTH the green pre-merge and red post-merge gate evidence snapshots, emits `post_merge_gate_failed` then `issue_blocked`, and the issue is never moved to done/. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:2726 "post-merge re-gate RED -> integration reset to pre-merge sha + issue blocked + others continue".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.24] | The pre-merge gate evidence and post-merge gate evidence are both written to the SAME path (`<gatesDir>/<id>-gate.json`) — the post-merge run necessarily overwrites the pre-merge green record on disk. | `readGateEvidenceSnapshot` is called to CAPTURE the green record's content BEFORE the post-merge gate overwrites the file, so the block report embeds both verdicts' actual content (not two pointers to the same now-red file). | unit | covered-today: implied by :2726 test's assertion on both evidence blocks (verify test body confirms content, not just path, of `pre_merge_gate_evidence`/`post_merge_gate_evidence`).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.25] | The `resetHard` call itself FAILS (e.g. git reset errors, exits non-zero) while trying to revert a damaging merge. | The loop does NOT throw (would crash the scheduler and strand independent siblings); instead it writes a loud `CRITICAL` stderr line naming the exact sha and the failure, then STILL proceeds to write the integration-blocked report and mark the issue blocked — main may be left red, requiring manual intervention, but the run itself survives. | unit | GAP (the `reset.status !== 0` branch's stderr-CRITICAL path exists in code but no test forces `resetHard` to return a non-zero status and asserts the loop continues rather than crashing).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.26] | Post-merge-red block happens while OTHER independent issues are still running/pending in the same wave. | The blocked issue is added to `blocked` (never rescheduled) but `running.size` for the other in-flight issues is untouched; the scheduler's outer for-loop continues scheduling/awaiting the remaining independent issues normally. | integration | covered-today (partially): factory/dev-loop.test.ts:2726 test name says "... + others continue" — confirm test body actually asserts a sibling's verified outcome, else GAP for the "others continue" half specifically.
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.27] | A dependent issue Z (declared `depends_on: [X]`) where X is the one that gets post-merge-reverted-and-blocked: Z must never become "ready" (X is never in `doneIds` since it was never moved to done/, because `moveIssueToDone` runs only after the post-merge gate passes). | `computeDoneIds`/`dependenciesSatisfied` correctly exclude Z from `computeReadySet` for the remainder of the run (X is permanently in `blocked`, not `doneIds`); Z is never scheduled, matching the intent that a reverted issue's dependents must not build on unlanded code. | unit | GAP (no test constructs a dependent issue behind a post-merge-reverted one and asserts it never gets scheduled for the rest of the run).

### Worktree leftovers after crash

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.28] | The dev-loop process is killed (or crashes) mid-run while issue `foo`'s worktree at `.vivicy-worktrees/foo` and branch `vivicy/foo` exist but the issue never integrated. On the NEXT `runLoopParallel` invocation (via supervisor relaunch), `defaultCreateWorktree("foo", ...)` is called again. | The stale worktree + branch are force-removed (`git worktree remove --force`, `git branch -D`, and an `rmSync` fallback if the dir still exists) BEFORE creating a fresh worktree branched from the CURRENT integration HEAD — so a crash never leaves a permanently stuck or duplicated worktree, and `foo` restarts clean from the latest integration state (potentially re-doing lost work, but never corrupting git state). | integration | GAP (no test simulates an actual leftover worktree directory + branch on disk and asserts `defaultCreateWorktree` cleans it up — the function's docstring documents this idempotent-resume behavior but it is exercised only implicitly via fake `createWorktree` steps in the parallel tests, never the real git-backed default).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.29] | The supervisor (`dev-loop-supervised.ts`) is the actual crash-recovery mechanism: `nextSupervisorAction` decides "relaunch" whenever `done < total`, `blocked === 0`, `attempt < maxRelaunches`, and `stall < stallLimit` — i.e. a crashed child (any exit code/signal) is relaunched by default UNLESS one of those stop conditions holds. | `main()`'s for-loop spawns `dev-loop.ts` again via `spawnSync`, which re-enters `runLoop`/`runLoopParallel` from the resumable on-disk state (done/, ledger) — worktree leftovers from the killed prior child are exactly case .28's cleanup path. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:713 "supervisor relaunches while progressing and stops on done/block/stall/cap" (covers `nextSupervisorAction`'s pure decision function; does not itself exercise the worktree-cleanup-on-relaunch integration).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.30] | A crash leaves the O_EXCL integration-lock file (`<gatesDir>/.integration.lock`) on disk with a stale pid (the process that held it is dead) or an old `epoch_ms` (> `INTEGRATION_LOCK_STALE_MS` = 120s). | `reclaimStaleExclusiveLock` detects `tooOld` (elapsed since `epoch_ms`/mtime) or `dead` (pid no longer alive per `isPidAlive`) and unlinks the stale lock so the next `acquireExclusiveLock` call succeeds instead of hanging until its own 30s timeout. | unit | GAP (no test writes a stale lock file with an old epoch/dead pid and asserts `acquireExclusiveLock` reclaims it rather than timing out — the reclaim function exists but is not directly unit-tested).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.31] | A crash leaves the lock file with a LIVE pid (a genuinely still-running sibling process, e.g. two supervisor instances launched against the same target root by mistake). | `acquireExclusiveLock` busy-waits (`busyWaitMs(25)`) in a loop up to a 30-second deadline; if the lock is never released it throws `"Timed out acquiring integration lock"` — the run fails loudly rather than silently corrupting the git index by proceeding without the lock. | unit | GAP (no test constructs a genuinely-live competing lock holder and asserts the 30s-timeout throw path — only the in-process mutex ordering is exercised, not the cross-process file-lock contention/timeout).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.32] | Two SEPARATE supervisor processes are started against the same target root (e.g. the UI's `startSupervisor` races a CLI-invoked `vivicy dev`). | `lib/control.ts`'s `claimRunLock` (O_EXCL `wx` write of `run-state.json`) refuses the second start with `ControlError("a supervised run is already active", "already_running")` if the first's pid is alive; if the first's recorded pid is dead, the lock is cleared and the second claim succeeds. This is a DIFFERENT, higher-level lock than the in-repo integration lock (.30/.31) — it prevents two whole dev-loop supervisors AGAINST THE SAME target, not just two merges; the lock is per-project, so two DIFFERENT target roots may each start a supervisor concurrently (legal cross-project parallelism). | unit | GAP (no test in control.test.ts exercises the concurrent-claim race for `claimRunLock`/`startSupervisor` itself — only inspected the file listing, not a live "two callers race to start" scenario).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.33] | `ensureWorktreesIgnored` must add `.vivicy-worktrees/` to `.gitignore` exactly once, idempotently, across many relaunches — a crash-and-relaunch cycle must never duplicate the gitignore entry or fail because it's already present. | Repeated calls are no-ops once the entry exists (`if (lines.includes(entry) || lines.includes(cfg.worktreesDir!)) return;`), so a worktree dir never shows up as a stray "untracked" change after a crash+resume, keeping `assertCleanTree`'s dirty-tree refusal from false-triggering on worktree leftovers. | unit | GAP (no direct test of `ensureWorktreesIgnored`'s idempotence across repeated calls with a pre-existing `.gitignore`).

### Integration lock contention

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.34] | Two issues settle (green worktree, ready to integrate) at nearly the same wall-clock moment — one is mid-merge (holds the file lock) while the other's `withIntegrationLock` call is queued. | The IN-PROCESS mutex (`integrationMutexes`, keyed by lock path) serializes the two calls deterministically within the same Node process BEFORE either touches the file lock, so there is never a moment where two `fn()` bodies run concurrently even though the OS-level EEXIST race would otherwise be possible; the second caller's critical section (merge -> done-move -> commit) only starts after the first's fully completes (success or throw). | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:1897 "runLoopParallel keeps the ledger consistent under many concurrent completions (no lost events)" exercises the serialization's OBSERVABLE effect (no lost/duplicate ledger writes), though it does not directly assert lock acquisition ORDER.
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.35] | The critical section function `fn` passed to `withIntegrationLock` THROWS (e.g. an unexpected error during merge/commit). | Both the file lock (`finally { unlinkSync(lockPath) }`) and the in-process mutex (`finally { release(); }`) are released even on throw, so a single failing integration attempt never permanently wedges the lock for subsequent callers; the throw propagates to the caller (`runOne`'s outer try/catch or the scheduler's `.catch` on the task promise), blocking only that one issue. | unit | GAP (no test forces the `fn` passed into `withIntegrationLock` to throw and asserts both the file lock and the mutex are released for a subsequent caller — the "queue never poisoned" comment on `integrationMutexes.set` documents intent but isn't directly unit-tested at this level, only the composed runLoopParallel-level unexpected-error path (`.catch` at line ~3422) is tested elsewhere for "unexpected error blocks only that issue").
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.36] | Worktree CREATION (not just integration) is serialized under the integration lock too (`created = await withIntegrationLock(cfg, () => wt.createWorktree(issue));`) — so a batch of N issues starting up simultaneously must create their N worktrees one at a time (never two `git worktree add` calls racing on the same `.git/worktrees` metadata). | All N worktree-creation calls interleave safely with any concurrent integration/done-move calls from OTHER already-running issues, because they all funnel through the same single lock — worktree setup for issue #5 never corrupts the git metadata while issue #2 is mid-merge. | integration | GAP (existing multi-issue parallel tests use FAKE `createWorktree` steps that don't touch real git worktree metadata or the real lock timing at scale; no test asserts worktree-add calls are serialized against concurrent merges specifically).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.37] | Lock is held for longer than `INTEGRATION_LOCK_STALE_MS` (120s) by a genuinely slow but still-alive process (e.g. a very large repo's `git merge` taking >120s) — a WAITING second caller must NOT prematurely reclaim it as stale just because the wall-clock threshold passed, since `reclaimStaleExclusiveLock` also checks `dead` (pid liveness), not just staleness. | `!tooOld && !dead` returns false only when BOTH the pid is alive AND under the staleness threshold is false — i.e. `tooOld` alone (pid still alive) is INSUFFICIENT to reclaim; re-reading the code: `if (!tooOld && !dead) return false;` means reclaim happens when `tooOld || dead` — so a still-alive-but-slow (>120s) holder's lock WOULD be reclaimed by staleness alone, a potential correctness gap allowing two processes to both believe they hold the merge lock simultaneously if a merge genuinely runs >120s. | unit | GAP — flag as a real design risk to verify against actual code intent, not just a missing test: confirm whether `tooOld` alone reclaiming (ignoring `dead`) is intentional (assumes merges never legitimately take >120s) or a latent bug under a very large repository.

### Zero-loss verification of merged code

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.38] | Across a full parallel run with many concurrent completions (verified + blocked mixed), every `issue_started`/`gate_passed`/`issue_blocked`/etc. ledger event actually lands — none is lost to a write race between the ledger's own O_EXCL+revision-CAS writer and the scheduler's concurrency. | The recorded ledger's event count / per-issue final states exactly match the expected settled outcomes for every issue in the run (no missing, no duplicated, no corrupted state). | integration | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:1897 "runLoopParallel keeps the ledger consistent under many concurrent completions (no lost events)".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.39] | The code that actually lands in `done/` + on the integration branch for a given issue is EXACTLY what passed the POST-MERGE gate (not the pre-merge worktree gate) — i.e. `gate_passed`'s `evidence_refs` for a parallel issue reference `postMergeGate.evidenceRel`, never the pre-merge worktree evidence, since the pre-merge evidence describes code that may have been altered by the merge (frozen-artifact resets, or genuine merge-driven changes). | The emitted `gate_passed` event for a parallel issue always carries the POST-MERGE evidence path; the ledger (and downstream map/status UI) never claims a node verified based on stale pre-merge evidence. | unit | covered-today (implicit): the code at dev-loop.ts:3351-3359 explicitly comments "supersedes the pre-merge worktree run" and wires `evidence_refs: [postMergeGate.evidenceRel]`; confirm an existing test asserts this specific evidence path (not just that SOME gate_passed fires) — likely GAP for the specific assertion, though the mechanism is exercised.
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.40] | A worktree branch's frozen-artifact edits (canonical/baselines/requirements/architecture-map.yml/issue-index) are discarded (reset to the integration HEAD's version) BEFORE the merge, so the merged code that lands is byte-identical to integration HEAD on every frozen path even if the implementer agent misbehaved and edited one. | `defaultResetWorktreeFrozenArtifacts` neutralizes the edit as a no-op at merge time; the merged tree's frozen paths exactly match pre-merge integration HEAD, never the agent's edit — "zero loss" here means zero SPEC-DRIFT loss, not zero-code-loss (the agent's frozen edit is deliberately discarded, which is itself the correctness property). | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:1982 "defaultResetWorktreeFrozenArtifacts drops a worktree's frozen-artifact edit while keeping legit src changes" + :2027 "two parallel branches both editing a frozen artifact integrate WITHOUT a conflict".
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.41] | `frozenIntegrationPaths` covers exactly the FROZEN extraction corpus (canonical/, baselines/, requirements/, architecture-map.yml, issue-index path) and explicitly EXCLUDES the loop's own lifecycle files (issues/, done/, ledger, gates, reports, transcripts) and `package.json` — a legitimate new dependency an implementer adds must NEVER be silently discarded by the frozen-artifact reset (that would be a real "zero-loss" violation: losing legitimate merged work). | The reset function never touches `package.json` or any loop-lifecycle path even though those ARE shared/orchestration-adjacent files, preserving legitimate cross-worktree contributions like a new runtime dependency. | unit | covered-today: factory/dev-loop.test.ts:1958 "frozenIntegrationPaths covers the locked extraction corpus, not loop lifecycle files".

### Done-move commits racing

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.42] | Two issues both reach the "move to done/ + commit" step under the SAME integration lock at nearly the same time (one right after the other, serialized by the lock) — `moveIssueToDone` (rename issue file into `done/`, patch issue-index path) then `commitDoneMove` (`git add` the orchestration paths + `git commit`) for issue A must fully complete (rename on disk + git commit) BEFORE issue B's `moveIssueToDone`/`commitDoneMove` starts, so B's commit never silently folds in or races A's uncommitted rename. | Because both calls happen INSIDE the same `withIntegrationLock` critical section per issue, they are strictly serialized; B's `git add -A`-equivalent (`git add -- <paths>`) only ever sees A's rename already committed (A's critical section fully returned before B's `withIntegrationLock` callback starts). | unit | covered-today (implicit via .34/.38): no test isolates a "done-move commit race" specifically distinct from the general "many concurrent completions" ledger-consistency test — likely a GAP for a targeted assertion that git history shows N distinct done-move commits in the observed serialization order, not just N ledger entries.
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.43] | `moveIssueToDone` runs BEFORE `commitDoneMove`/the verified-emit, so a kill exactly between the rename and the commit must never leave an issue's file physically in `done/` (renamed) while the ledger/commit never recorded it — on resume, `computeDoneIds`'s `doneFileNames.has(`${issue.id}.md`)` check treats the PHYSICAL presence in done/ as authoritative regardless of the ledger, so a half-completed move-then-crash is still correctly resumed as "done" (the rename is the atomic commit point for resume purposes, not the git commit). | On relaunch, the issue is correctly classified `done` (via the done/ file check) even if the accompanying git commit never happened; the next `git add -A`/`commitDoneMove` on ANY subsequent issue would fold in the still-uncommitted done/ move (recovering it), or if no further issue runs, the done/ move is simply left uncommitted on disk until the next opportunity. | unit | GAP (no test kills the process — or simulates the equivalent via injected steps that throw between `moveIssueToDone` and `commitDoneMove` — and asserts the RESUME behavior correctly treats the physically-moved file as done despite the missing commit).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.44] | In the SEQUENTIAL path (`runLoop`), the ordering is also move-before-commit (`moveIssueToDone(issue, cfg); resolvedSteps.commit(issue, cfg);`) — the same half-crash-safety property as .43 applies to the non-parallel path, and should be verified symmetrically (both loops share the invariant, but nothing enforces they can't drift apart in a future edit). | Sequential resume after a kill between move and commit also correctly reports the issue done (same done/-file-presence check). | unit | GAP (mirrors .43 for the sequential path specifically; a future refactor could accidentally reorder one path and not the other without a test catching it).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.45] | `commitDoneMove`'s `git commit` is tolerated as a no-op when nothing is staged (e.g. a resumed run whose done/ move was already committed by a prior process before the crash) — the function's docstring says "A no-op `git commit` (nothing staged) is tolerated," implying the commit's exit code is deliberately not checked/thrown-on. | A resumed re-attempt at `commitDoneMove` for an already-committed issue does not error the run (git's own "nothing to commit" non-zero exit is silently absorbed, since the code never inspects `spawnSync(...).status`). | unit | GAP (no test re-invokes `commitDoneMove` twice for the same issue and asserts the second call doesn't throw/blow up the loop).

### Cross-cutting: settings <-> factory parity for parallelism knobs

- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.46] | `lib/settings.ts`'s `MIN_PARALLEL/MAX_PARALLEL = [1, 12]` and `factory/dev-loop.ts`'s `MIN_CONCURRENCY/MAX_CONCURRENCY = [1, 12]` are two independently-defined constants that MUST agree (defense-in-depth per the code comments) — a future edit to one without the other would silently desync the UI's allowed range from the factory's actual clamp. | Both constants pairs equal `[1, 12]`; no test currently CROSS-CHECKS the two modules' constants against each other (each file only tests its own copy). | unit | GAP (a cheap, high-value regression test: assert `MIN_PARALLEL === MIN_CONCURRENCY && MAX_PARALLEL === MAX_CONCURRENCY` by importing both modules, catching drift immediately rather than via a subtle production behavior mismatch).
- [cross.chaos-parallel-merge.47] | `dev-status.ts` surfaces `active_items` from the ledger for the UI while a parallel run has multiple issues concurrently "active" (not yet done, not yet blocked) — the status read path must show ALL currently-running parallel issues, not just one, during a 12-way fan-out. | `readDevStatus`'s returned `active` array reflects every issue with an in-flight ledger state at the moment of the read, matching the actual concurrency at that instant. | e2e-ui | GAP (no e2e-ui test polls `/api/status` or the SSE stream during an actual multi-issue-parallel run and asserts multiple simultaneous active items render in the UI — existing UI tests (pipeline-widget, control.spec.ts) exercise single-issue/sequential states only).

---

**Case count: 47** (`cross.chaos-parallel-merge.1` through `.47`)

## Cross: cross-chaos-process

Grounded in: `lib/control.ts`, `lib/runtime-dir.ts`, `lib/spawner.ts`, `lib/vivi.ts`, `lib/upload.ts`, `factory/dev-loop-supervised.ts`, `factory/dev-loop.ts`, `factory/progress-ledger.ts`, `factory/atomic-write.ts`, `factory/target-root.ts`, `factory/install-skills.ts`, `factory/cr-apply.ts`, `app/api/control/{start,stop,resume,retry-stage}/route.ts`, `e2e/control.spec.ts`.

Key mechanism inventory that every scenario below cites:
- **run-state.json** (per-project `<runtime>/projects/<key>/run-state.json`; `lib/control.ts` `claimRunLock`/`isRunActive`/`readRunState`): O_EXCL `wx` claim, liveness = `spawner.isAlive(pid)` (real: `process.kill(pid,0)`), stale lock auto-cleared on next claim attempt only (no background sweep).
- **skills-install.lock** (per-project `<runtime>/projects/<key>/skills-install.lock`; `lib/control.ts` `claimSkillsLock`/`isSkillsLockLive`): same O_EXCL + pid-liveness pattern, independent of run-state.json.
- **skills-report.json phase liveness** (`lib/control.ts` `isSkillsInstallInFlight`, `SKILLS_STALE_MS=15min`): a second liveness signal for the SAME stage, based on `updated_at` staleness rather than pid — the lock and the report can disagree.
- **.integration.lock** (`factory/dev-loop.ts` `withIntegrationLock`/`acquireExclusiveLock`/`reclaimStaleExclusiveLock`, `INTEGRATION_LOCK_STALE_MS=120s`): in-process async mutex + O_EXCL file lock, reclaimed on staleness OR dead pid; 30s acquire timeout.
- **progress-ledger.json.lock** (`factory/progress-ledger.ts` `acquireLock`/`reclaimStaleLock`, `LOCK_STALE_MS=30s`, `LOCK_ACQUIRE_TIMEOUT_MS=10s`) + revision CAS (`CAS_MAX_ATTEMPTS=50`).
- **dev-loop-supervisor.json** (`factory/dev-loop-supervised.ts` `writeState`): heartbeat file, no lock of its own — liveness is inferred by the UI/CLI from `run-state.json`'s pid, not this file's pid.
- **atomicWriteJson** (`factory/atomic-write.ts`): temp-file + rename; only atomic within one filesystem.
- Worktrees under `.vivicy-worktrees/<issue-id>` (`factory/dev-loop.ts` `defaultCreateWorktree`): idempotent create (force-removes stale worktree/branch first).
- The Next server and the factory process are **fully decoupled**: `startSupervisor`/`startSkillsInstall` use `spawner.spawnDetached`, so killing the Next (UI) process never touches the detached child; only `stopSupervisor` (an explicit API call) sends SIGTERM to the recorded pid.

---

### A. Next server killed while EXTRACTION runs (runExtract / retry-stage extract)

- [cross.chaos-process.1] | Next server process is killed (SIGKILL) mid-`await spawner.run(...)` inside `runExtract` (`lib/control.ts:518-552`), i.e. mid the SYNCHRONOUS-from-the-route `extract-issues.ts` child. | The route's `run()` is a *foreground* `spawner.run` (not detached) launched via `nodeSpawner.run` (child_process spawn awaited by the killed Next process) — killing the parent typically also kills the child (no `spawnDetached`, no independent process group survival is documented for `run()`, only for `spawnDetached`); extraction is left mid-write with whatever `extraction-status.json` phase it last flushed (e.g. "authoring"/"fixing"/"validating"). On UI reattach, `getExtractionStatus()` reads that stale in-flight phase back verbatim (no run lock guards extraction — only the supervised dev-loop and skills install have locks); a fresh POST to `/api/control/extract` (or retry-stage `extract`) simply reruns `runExtract`, which re-enters `extract-issues.ts` — the orchestrator's own internal idempotence (freeze-reuse, bounded retries) must carry resumption, since `lib/control.ts` provides no extraction-specific lock. | e2e-process | GAP — no lock exists to test double-launch of extraction; `route.test.ts` for extract only covers the fake-spawner happy/blocked path, never a killed-mid-run + reattach sequence. |
- [cross.chaos-process.2] | Two browser tabs (or UI + CLI) both POST `/api/control/extract` (or `retry-stage {stage:"extract"}`) concurrently — there is no `claimRunLock`-equivalent for extraction. | Both requests race into `spawner.run` for `extract-issues.ts` against the SAME target root; `assertRealCanonical` passes for both; nothing in `lib/control.ts` serializes this (unlike `startSupervisor`/`startSkillsInstall`). Whatever mutual-exclusion exists must come from inside `extract-issues.ts` itself (e.g. its own freeze/baseline file writes) — not visible/grounded in the files read for this matrix. | e2e-process | GAP — the whole file-set audited here has NO run-state guard on extract; this is a genuine concurrency hole worth flagging (see critical GAPs). |
- [cross.chaos-process.3] | Next server killed AFTER `extract-issues.ts` wrote `extraction-status.json` phase `"green"` but BEFORE the route's `Response.json` was ever sent. | `readExtractionStatus`/`getExtractionStatus` (`lib/control.ts:588-612`) is a pure disk read, so a UI reattach (new Next process or the same one restarted) sees the true "green" state immediately — no data is lost because the source of truth is the file, not the route's in-memory response. | integration | GAP |

### B. Next server killed while the SUPERVISED DEV-LOOP runs (sequential, N=1)

- [cross.chaos-process.4] | Next server (UI) is killed while `startSupervisor` has already detached `dev-loop-supervised.ts` (`lib/control.ts:374-431`, `spawner.spawnDetached`). | The detached supervisor keeps running (that is the entire point of `spawnDetached`/`nohup`-style launch documented in `factory/dev-loop-supervised.ts:11-16`); `run-state.json`'s pid is the SUPERVISOR's pid (patched in at `lib/control.ts:428`), independent of the Next process's pid, so a Next-process kill has zero effect on `isRunActive`. | e2e-process | GAP — no automated test kills the Next dev server and asserts the factory keeps running; `control.spec.ts` only exercises Stop-via-UI, never an out-of-band Next kill. |
- [cross.chaos-process.5] | UI reattaches (new Next process boots) while the supervisor is still alive. | `readRunState()` (`lib/control.ts:290-298`) reads the persisted lock unconditionally; `isRunActive(spawner)` calls `spawner.isAlive(state.pid)` against the REAL pid via `nodeSpawner`'s `process.kill(pid,0)`-style check (grounded in `lib/spawner.ts`'s `fakeSpawner.isAlive` mirroring the real contract) — since the supervisor process is a genuinely different OS process than the Next server, its liveness is independent of Next's restart; the UI's status pill and Stop button correctly reflect "running" on first status poll after reattach. | e2e-process | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.6] | Machine reboot (both Next AND the detached supervisor die together, OS-level). | On restart, nothing auto-relaunches either process (no daemon/systemd/launchd hook is present in the read files); `run-state.json` still names the now-dead pid. First `isRunActive` call (e.g. via `/api/status` -> `readDevStatus`) calls `spawner.isAlive(deadPid)`, gets false, and `clearRunState()` (`lib/control.ts:338-341`) removes the stale lock — UI now correctly shows "idle"/Resume available, and `startSupervisor(spawner,"resume")` can claim a fresh lock; the underlying dev-loop resumes from `done/` + the ledger per the supervisor's documented resumability (`factory/dev-loop-supervised.ts:5-7`). | e2e-process | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.7] | Reboot happens mid-`.integration.lock` hold (a parallel-loop merge was in flight) or mid `progress-ledger.json.lock` hold. | Both locks are file-based with pid+timestamp bodies and are reclaimed lazily: `.integration.lock` is stale after `INTEGRATION_LOCK_STALE_MS=120_000` OR when `owner.pid` is dead (`reclaimStaleExclusiveLock`, `factory/dev-loop.ts:2311-2330`); `progress-ledger.json.lock` likewise after `LOCK_STALE_MS=30_000` or a dead pid (`reclaimStaleLock`, `factory/progress-ledger.ts:279-309`). After reboot the owning pid is certainly dead, so the VERY NEXT acquire attempt reclaims it regardless of age — no operator action needed. A worktree left mid-merge is reclaimed by `defaultCreateWorktree`'s force-remove-then-recreate (`factory/dev-loop.ts:2039-2062`) on the next scheduling pass for that issue, and any half-written ledger is protected by `atomicWriteJson`'s rename-into-place (so no partial JSON is ever visible) though a merge-in-progress could still leave the git working tree with an uncommitted merge that needs no lockfile at all to reconcile (git itself refuses a second `git worktree add` onto a MERGE_HEAD tree until resolved). | e2e-process | GAP |

### C. Next server killed while DEV-LOOP PARALLEL (N=2..12) runs

- [cross.chaos-process.8] | Next server killed while `runLoopParallel` (`factory/dev-loop.ts:3127-3452`) — itself running inside the DETACHED supervised child, not the Next process — has several worktrees in flight (maxParallel between 2 and 12). | No effect on the parallel loop: it is a child of the detached supervisor, several process boundaries away from the Next server; `running`/`runningIssueById` state lives in that child's memory and is untouched. UI reattach re-reads `run-state.json` (still the supervisor's pid) and `dev-status.ts` output, which derives progress from the ledger + `done/` + `-blocked.json` files on disk (shared state, per `factory/dev-loop.ts:3121-3126`), so the parallel run's true progress is visible immediately. | e2e-process | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.9] | The DETACHED SUPERVISOR's own child (`dev-loop.ts`, running `runLoopParallel`) crashes mid-merge — after `wt.captureHead()` but before the `moveIssueToDone`/ledger emit/commit sequence completes (`factory/dev-loop.ts:3339-3360`) — and `dev-loop-supervised.ts` relaunches it. | `moveIssueToDone` happens BEFORE the orchestration commit specifically so "a kill between them never leaves an issue committed but missing from done/" (comment at `factory/dev-loop.ts:3339-3341`); on relaunch the new `dev-loop.ts` invocation re-reads the issue index + ledger + `done/` from disk and simply re-schedules whatever is not done and not blocked — the merge itself is git-durable (a completed `git merge --no-ff` is a real commit) so no re-merge is attempted for an issue whose merge commit already landed even if the done/-move+ledger-emit+commit sequence after it was interrupted; a NEW worktree for that same issue id would be idempotently recreated (`defaultCreateWorktree` force-removes any stale worktree/branch first) only if the issue is somehow still schedulable, which the done-file-and-ledger read should prevent once caught up. | integration | GAP — `factory/dev-loop.test.ts` should be checked for a targeted kill-between-merge-and-commit unit test; not confirmed present from this file list alone. |
- [cross.chaos-process.10] | Two UI tabs both click "Run" (`startSupervisor`) simultaneously while N=12 parallel is configured, racing `claimRunLock`. | `claimRunLock` (`lib/control.ts:313-336`) is atomic via `wx`: the first writer wins; the second's `writeFileSync(...,{flag:"wx"})` throws EEXIST, falls into the `isRunActive` check, sees the just-claimed placeholder pid (`process.pid`, the Next server's OWN pid, still alive since it's the very process handling the request) and throws `ControlError("already_running")` — surfaced as HTTP 409 by `/api/control/start`. No double-supervisor is ever spawned even under a tight race, because the placeholder-pid trick keeps the lock "live" through the check-then-spawn window (documented at `lib/control.ts:388-390`). | integration | `lib/control.test.ts` "refuses a double start while a run is active (single-run lock)" (line 122) + "holds the lock atomically before the spawn returns" (line 157) — covers this at the unit level with a fake spawner; a REAL concurrent-race (two actual HTTP requests) is still e2e-process GAP. |
- [cross.chaos-process.11] | UI (Stop button) and CLI (`vivicy stop` — not read in this pass, inferred from the CLI↔app parity doc comment `lib/control.ts:9-23`) both attempt to stop the SAME run concurrently. | `stopSupervisor` (`lib/control.ts:437-445`) reads `run-state.json` once; if both callers read it before either clears it, both call `spawner.killGroup(pid,"SIGTERM")` (idempotent — killing an already-dead process group returns `false` per the `Spawner.killGroup` contract, `lib/control.ts:100`) and both call `clearRunState()` (idempotent — `rmSync` guarded by `existsSync`, `lib/control.ts:338-341`); the second caller gets the same 200 response describing the same pid — no error, no corruption, just a slightly misleading "stopped" ack for whichever call lost the race (never surfaced as a 409 the way `already_running` is, since there's no re-check-after-race here). | integration | GAP — worth a dedicated unit test asserting the SECOND concurrent `stopSupervisor` call does not throw. |

### D. SKILLS INSTALL — Next kill / dual lock disagreement

- [cross.chaos-process.12] | Next server killed right after `startSkillsInstall` (`lib/control.ts:752-786`) detaches `install-skills.ts` but the skills-install.lock write at line 784 (`writeFileSync(skillsLockPath(), ...)` — the FINAL patch-in-real-pid write, done OUTSIDE the try/catch that wraps only the spawn) somehow fails (e.g. disk-full, see G-scenario). | The lock still contains the PLACEHOLDER `process.pid` (the Next server's own, now-dead, pid) rather than the real installer pid, because the patch write at line 784 is unguarded — if it throws, the exception propagates out of `startSkillsInstall` uncaught (no try/catch around it), so the caller (the route) sees a 500, yet the detached installer process is ALREADY RUNNING (the spawn succeeded) with the lock still pointing at the dead Next pid. Next `isSkillsLockLive` check sees `spawner.isAlive(deadNextPid)` = false, `clearSkillsLock()` fires, and a SECOND install can be started while the first is still running — because the write-failure path was never designed to also kill the just-spawned child. | e2e-process | GAP — this is a real correctness gap (see critical GAPs below); no test in `control.test.ts` exercises a throwing final `writeFileSync`. |
- [cross.chaos-process.13] | The skills-install.lock (pid-based) and skills-report.json's `phase`+`updated_at` (time-based) DISAGREE: the installer process is alive (lock live) but has been silently hung (e.g. blocked on a network fetch to skills.sh with no timeout past `AbortSignal.timeout(20_000)` per call, but stuck on a git/npx step, `factory/install-skills.ts:420-425`, `441-444`) for over `SKILLS_STALE_MS=15min`. | `claimSkillsLock`/`isSkillsLockLive` (pid-based) would refuse a new claim (process genuinely alive) even though `isSkillsInstallInFlight` (report-based, `lib/control.ts:665-671`) would independently judge the SAME install as "stale" past 15 minutes if only the report were consulted. Since `startSkillsInstall` calls BOTH `isSkillsInstallInFlight` (line 761) AND `claimSkillsLock` (line 764) in sequence, and the pid-liveness gate is checked SECOND (inside `claimSkillsLock`) and wins by throwing `already_running`, a genuinely wedged-but-alive installer can never be restarted by the UI even though the report looks abandoned — the two liveness definitions are not unified, and the more conservative (pid-alive) one is authoritative. | integration | GAP — dual-lock disagreement between report staleness and pid liveness is unexercised; flag as a design tension, not just a missing test. |
- [cross.chaos-process.14] | Runtime dir (`.vivicy-runtime/`, holding the per-project `projects/<key>/{run-state.json,skills-install.lock}` locks) is DELETED mid-run (e.g. `rm -rf .vivicy-runtime`) while both a supervised dev-loop AND a skills install are live. | Both detached child processes are UNAFFECTED (they were spawned with `cwd: factoryRoot`, not depending on the runtime dir's continued existence for their own execution — though `devEnv` passes `VIVICY_RUNTIME_DIR` for notify() appends, `lib/control.ts:281-287`, so in-flight notifications silently start failing/no-op'ing once the dir is gone, per `notify()`'s presumed best-effort contract). The NEXT `isRunActive`/`isSkillsInstallInFlight` check on the UI side sees NO lock file at all (not "stale", just absent) and reports idle/not-in-flight — so the UI would offer "Run" and "Install skills" again even though BOTH are still actually running headless, racing a THIRD process into the same target undetected until the orphaned real processes' writes (ledger, done/, skills-report.json) start colliding with the new ones. | e2e-process | GAP — critical: deleting the runtime dir defeats every lock in this codebase simultaneously; no test covers it. |
- [cross.chaos-process.15] | `skills-install.lock`'s FINAL pid-patch write races a concurrent reader in `isSkillsLockLive` (read of a lock file mid-write). | `readSkillsLock` (`lib/control.ts:692-699`) wraps `JSON.parse` in try/catch and returns `null` on any parse failure (including a torn/partial read of a lock file mid-`writeFileSync`), and `isSkillsLockLive` treats `null` as "not live" (`lib/control.ts:705-711`) — a torn read during the placeholder-to-real-pid patch could transiently make a genuinely active install look unlocked, letting a second claim through in that narrow window. `writeFileSync` is not atomic (unlike `atomicWriteJson`'s temp+rename pattern used elsewhere) so this is a real, if narrow, TOCTOU gap specific to the lock files (both `run-state.json`'s `updateRunState` and `skills-install.lock`'s final patch use plain non-atomic `writeFileSync`, `lib/control.ts:301-303`, `784`). | unit | GAP |

### E. VIVI CHAT — Next kill mid-turn

- [cross.chaos-process.16] | Next server killed mid-`await spawner.run(...)` inside `runViviTurn` (`lib/vivi.ts:543-559`), i.e. mid the (synchronous, non-detached) `vivi-turn.ts` leg. | Same class as extraction (scenario 1): this is a FOREGROUND spawn from the Next process, so killing Next likely kills the leg too (no `spawnDetached`); the user's turn was already durably appended to the transcript BEFORE the spawn (`appendTurn(sessionId,{role:"user",...})` at `lib/vivi.ts:519`, explicitly "so the transcript is durable even if the agent leg dies"), so on UI reattach the conversation is never silently lost — but the assistant's reply for that turn never gets recorded (no `vivi` role turn appended), and any partial `.vivicy` writes the leg made before being killed are left in place with NO byte-level restore, because `diffVivicy`/`restoreSnapshot` only run in the code AFTER the (now-never-returning) `spawner.run` call — a killed leg's partial writes are NEVER rolled back (the rollback machinery only triggers on a violation detected in a COMPLETED run, not a crash). | e2e-process | GAP — this is a real gap: `snapshotVivicyBytes`/`restoreSnapshot` protect against a violating-but-completed turn, not a killed-mid-write one; the prompt/reply scratch files (`promptFile`/`replyFile`) are cleaned up in a `finally` around the spawn (`lib/vivi.ts:560-563` `finally { rmSync(promptFile...) }`) only if the AWAIT ITSELF returns/throws in-process — a hard kill of the whole Next process skips even that `finally`, leaving `<sessionId>.<turnToken>.prompt.txt` and possibly a half-flushed `.reply.txt` behind under `.vivicy-runtime/vivi/` forever (nothing ever sweeps orphaned turn-token scratch files). |
- [cross.chaos-process.17] | Two concurrent Vivi turns on the SAME session race (e.g. UI double-submit, or UI+CLI both driving the same session id). | The turn-scratch filenames are keyed by BOTH `sessionId` AND a freshly minted `turnToken = randomUUID()` (`lib/vivi.ts:529`), explicitly "so two concurrent turns on the SAME session never race on each other's prompt/reply scratch files" — so no file collision. However `appendTurn` (`lib/vivi.ts:149-154`) opens with `{flag:"a"}` (append) with no lock at all; two concurrent turns' `appendTurn` calls for the SAME session's user-turn-then-vivi-turn writes could interleave at the OS append level (POSIX single-writer append is normally atomic per write() syscall for small writes, but two full JS `writeFileSync` calls are two separate syscalls) — a genuine race could interleave user/assistant turns out of order in the JSONL, though each LINE itself stays intact (single `writeFileSync` call per turn). More seriously, BOTH turns independently snapshot `.vivicy` BEFORE their own spawn and diff AFTER — if turn A writes a canonical file and turn B's snapshot predates A's write but B's diff runs AFTER A's write lands, B would see A's file as "new" and misattribute it as ITS OWN write (or, worse, as a violation if outside B's allowed dirs) — there is no locking around `.vivicy` snapshot/diff at all (unlike the ledger's O_EXCL+CAS or the integration lock), so concurrent turns on the same target (even different sessions) can corrupt each other's allowlist enforcement. | integration | GAP — significant: `runViviTurn` has zero mutual exclusion around the snapshot/spawn/diff window; flag as a critical GAP. |
- [cross.chaos-process.18] | Next server killed AFTER `applySkillsDirective` (`lib/vivi.ts:652-664`) has called `startSkillsInstall` (successfully claiming the skills lock and detaching the installer) but BEFORE the vivi turn's own `appendTurn` for the `vivi` role completes. | The skills install is a fully independent detached process by this point (unaffected by the Next kill, same as scenario 12); but the transcript never records that THIS turn triggered it (the `vivi`-role turn with the directive's resulting reply text is never appended) — on UI reattach, the user sees their message with no assistant reply for that turn (per `readTranscript`'s tolerant per-line parsing, `lib/vivi.ts:132-146`, the transcript file itself is fine, just missing the last line), while the skills install silently proceeds and eventually shows up in the Skills report/section independent of the chat UI. | e2e-process | GAP |

### F. IMPORT (S1-import) — Next kill mid-governance / mid-batch-write

- [cross.chaos-process.19] | Next server killed mid-`importDocuments` (`lib/import-docs.ts`) AFTER the governance scaffold is laid (`scaffoldProject` committed the skeleton) but BEFORE the batch is written. | The folder is governed with zero batches under `.vivicy/uploads/`; a re-POST of the same `targetDir` now hits the `already_governed` guard (the folder IS governed), so a killed import cannot silently re-scaffold — the acquisition is idempotent at the folder level and no partial batch is left consumable. | integration | GAP — no test kills the server between scaffold and batch write; the already-governed re-POST recovery path is unproven. |
- [cross.chaos-process.20] | Next server killed mid-batch-write inside `importDocuments`, after some accepted files are copied into `.vivicy/uploads/<batch-id>/` but before `manifest.json` is written. | `manifest.json` is deliberately the LAST write of an import (the batch-complete marker), so a batch dir without one is an interrupted, non-consumable batch the pipeline must skip — a killed import leaves a manifest-less (ignorable) partial batch, never a half-manifested one the pipeline would wrongly consume. Unlike the retired `applyUpload`, nothing is placed into the governed corpus (`.vivicy/canonical/**`) at import time, so there is no partial-corpus hazard. | e2e-process | GAP — no test asserts the pipeline skips a manifest-less batch dir; the completion-marker invariant is enforced only by write ordering. |
- [cross.chaos-process.21] | The batch dir (`.vivicy/uploads/<batch-id>/`) is deleted (e.g. the target tree is wiped, scenario 25) after an import returns but before the pipeline consumes it. | The next import mints a fresh, collision-safe `<batch-id>` and writes a new complete batch; the pipeline consumes the LATEST batch by id order, so a deleted older batch drops out of consideration rather than wedging anything — there is no cross-batch lock or staging id to leave dangling (the retired staging-dir model is gone). | integration | GAP |

### G. Disk-full / write-failure chaos on report files

- [cross.chaos-process.22] | Disk full (ENOSPC) during `atomicWriteJson` (`factory/atomic-write.ts:8-26`) for the progress ledger. | The temp-file `writeSync` throws; the `finally { closeSync(fd) }` still runs, but the subsequent `renameSync` is never reached — the function re-throws the original ENOSPC error up through `recordProgressEvent`'s CAS loop (`factory/progress-ledger.ts:224`), which is NOT itself wrapped in a catch, so the error propagates to the caller (`emit()` in `dev-loop.ts`, best-effort-wrapped in SOME call sites — e.g. `writeIntegrationBlock`'s emit is wrapped in try/catch, `factory/dev-loop.ts:3471-3482` — but NOT in the main `gate_passed`/`issue_completed` emit path inside `runOne`, `factory/dev-loop.ts:3351-3359`, which has no surrounding try/catch): a disk-full ledger write during the terminal "verified" emit would throw out of `runOne`'s async task, get caught by the scheduler's generic `.catch` (`factory/dev-loop.ts:3422-3428`, "An UNEXPECTED throw inside a task blocks only that issue"), and the issue is marked `blocked` with the ENOSPC message as `error` — technically handled (no crash), but SEMANTICALLY MISLEADING: an issue that fully passed its gate and merged onto main gets recorded as "blocked" purely because the ledger write ran out of disk, and the lock (`.integration.lock`) held for that critical section (line 2266's `withIntegrationLock` wraps this whole block) is released via its `finally` (line 2282-2288) so it does NOT deadlock the next issue — but the merge itself already landed on the integration branch even though the run reports "blocked", so `done/` and the ledger now DISAGREE with git history (the merge commit exists, but no `-blocked.json` reason names "disk full" specifically — it inherits whatever the generic scheduler catch stringifies). | integration | GAP — flag as a P3 "blind state" risk: a disk-full ledger write masks a fully successful merge as a blocked issue with a confusing reason. |
- [cross.chaos-process.23] | Disk-full during `writeFileSync` for `run-state.json`'s lock claim (`claimRunLock`, `lib/control.ts:317-336`) — the FIRST `wx` write throws ENOSPC (not EEXIST). | The `catch` block only re-throws when `code !== "EEXIST"` (line 321) — an ENOSPC error is NOT "EEXIST", so it re-throws immediately, propagating out of `claimRunLock` and `startSupervisor` uncaught by any ControlError wrapper (the try/catch around the SPAWN in `startSupervisor`, lines 409-425, is a SEPARATE try block that does not cover `claimRunLock`, called earlier at line 401) — the route's generic `catch` (`app/api/control/start/route.ts:19-27`) still turns this into a 500 with the raw ENOSPC message, so the user sees an honest (if unpolished) failure rather than a silent hang; no lock is left behind since the write never completed. | unit | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.24] | Disk-full during `stampChangeRequestApplied` or `defaultRecordReport` (`factory/cr-apply.ts:469-474`) mid the CR-apply chain, specifically after FREEZE succeeded but before the CR file is stamped `docs_applied`. | `applyChangeRequest`'s freeze step (`factory/cr-apply.ts:247-252`) is wrapped in try/catch and reports `blocked`/`"freeze"` phase on ANY throw including a disk-full inside `runFreeze`'s child `doc-baseline.ts` invocation; the STAMP step (256-271) is separately try/catch-guarded and reports `blocked`/`"stamp"` if `stampChangeRequestApplied` throws (e.g. disk-full mid-write) — leaving the CR at `accepted_current_build` (never wrongly advanced to `docs_applied` without the stamp actually landing) and the new baseline already frozen on disk; a retry of `cr-apply.ts --cr <id>` for the SAME CR would re-run APPLY (the applier leg edits canonical again, likely a no-op re-edit since the fold already landed) then re-FREEZE (bumping the patch version AGAIN from the same `previous_baseline_version`, since the CR's frontmatter was never updated) — producing a SECOND frozen baseline that supersedes the first, silently orphaning the first freeze rather than reusing it. Grounded in the "CR left accepted_current_build" comment pattern used throughout (`factory/cr-apply.ts:191`, `215-219`) confirming resume-from-accepted is the intended contract, but NOT confirming idempotent re-freeze avoidance. | integration | GAP — potential double-freeze-on-retry after a stamp failure; worth a dedicated resume test. |

### H. Target repo deleted/moved mid-run

- [cross.chaos-process.25] | Target repo directory is deleted (e.g. `rm -rf`) while the detached supervisor/dev-loop is mid-cycle. | Every disk operation in `dev-loop.ts` (`readJson`, `writeFileSync`, the git `spawnSync` calls) would start throwing ENOENT/`fatal: not a git repository` from that point forward; `resolveTargetRoot()` (`factory/target-root.ts:38-42`) only checks the ENV VAR is set/non-empty, never that the path still EXISTS on disk, so nothing fails fast at startup for an already-running loop — the sequential path's per-issue try/catch (mirroring the parallel path's, `factory/dev-loop.ts:3422-3428` for parallel; the sequential single-issue path likely has an analogous top-level catch, not fully traced in this pass) would surface a generic "unexpected error" per issue rather than a clean "target repo missing" diagnosis. Meanwhile the UI's `readDevStatus` (`lib/control.ts:451-477`) calls `resolveContext()` which DOES check `existsSync(targetRoot)` (line 455-457) and throws a clean `ControlError("missing_target")` — so the UI-facing surface fails cleanly while the headless loop likely thrashes with unclean per-issue errors until the supervisor's stall-detection (`nextSupervisorAction`'s `stall >= stallLimit`, `factory/dev-loop-supervised.ts:48-58`, default `STALL_LIMIT=3`) eventually calls it "stalled" and exits non-zero. | e2e-process | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.26] | Target repo is MOVED (renamed) to a new path mid-run, `VIVICY_TARGET_ROOT` / the UI's persisted target selection still points at the OLD path. | Same failure class as deletion (scenario 25) from the factory side (old path now ENOENT); from the UI side, `getTargetRoot()` (referenced but not read in this pass — persisted current-project selection under the runtime dir per `lib/control.ts`'s doc comment line 27) would need updating by the user via Settings before the UI can resolve a NEW valid `resolveContext()`; until then `readDevStatus`/every control verb throws `missing_target` cleanly. | e2e-process | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.27] | Target repo has git identity missing (no `user.name`/`user.email` configured) when `defaultCommit`/`commitApplied`/the freeze's underlying git commit runs. | Not directly read in this pass for the exact git-commit call sites' error handling, but `defaultCommitApplied` (`factory/cr-apply.ts:393-407`) checks `commit.status` and tolerates ONLY the specific "nothing to commit" message as success (line 402); any OTHER non-zero exit (which a missing git identity would produce, since `git commit` refuses without `user.email`) is treated as `{committed:false}`, propagating to `applyChangeRequest`'s `if (!committed.committed)` block at a `"commit"` phase (`factory/cr-apply.ts:244-246`) — reported honestly as `blocked`/`"commit"` rather than silently continuing to freeze a dirty/uncommitted tree (which `doc-baseline.ts`'s clean-tree guard would also independently refuse, per the "freeze failed" try/catch at lines 248-252). Parallel-loop's `defaultCommit` (`factory/dev-loop.ts:1883`+) very likely follows the same non-zero-exit-is-a-failure pattern though the exact block wasn't re-read in full in this pass. | integration | GAP |

### I. Stale-lock matrix (dead vs. alive pid, by lock type)

- [cross.chaos-process.28] | `run-state.json` names a DEAD pid (process crashed without SIGTERM cleanup) — the common "kill -9'd the wrong process" or OOM-killed supervisor case. | `isRunActive` (`lib/control.ts:348-354`) calls `spawner.isAlive(deadPid)` -> false -> `clearRunState()` -> returns false. A subsequent `startSupervisor` call's `claimRunLock` sees the EEXIST on its first `wx` write, calls `isRunActive` (false, and ALREADY clears the file as a side effect), then retries the `wx` write which now succeeds cleanly (no race window against itself since it's sequential in one call) — a completely clean recovery path, unit-tested already. | unit | `lib/control.test.ts` "allows a restart once the prior pid is no longer alive (stale lock)" (line 136). |
- [cross.chaos-process.29] | `run-state.json` names an ALIVE pid but belonging to an UNRELATED process (pid got reused by the OS after the real supervisor died, e.g. long-uptime machine, pid wraparound). | `isAlive`/`process.kill(pid,0)` has no way to distinguish "this pid IS my supervisor" from "this pid happens to be alive as some other program" — `isRunActive` would report `true` (falsely "running") and refuse a genuine restart with `already_running`, requiring a human to manually delete `run-state.json` (or wait for that unrelated pid to also exit) since there is no secondary identity check (e.g. a process-start-time or a command-line match) anywhere in `lib/control.ts`. | e2e-process | GAP — PID-reuse false-positive is an inherent design limitation, not just an untested path; worth flagging explicitly. |
- [cross.chaos-process.30] | `.integration.lock` is DEAD-pid-stale (parallel-loop child crashed while holding it) vs. ALIVE-but-past-`INTEGRATION_LOCK_STALE_MS` (a genuinely slow merge/gate exceeding 120s). | `reclaimStaleExclusiveLock` (`factory/dev-loop.ts:2311-2330`) reclaims on EITHER condition (`tooOld || dead`) — so a slow-but-alive holder (e.g. a merge-resolver leg legitimately taking >120s under heavy load) gets its lock FORCE-STOLEN by a waiting sibling even though it is still working, a genuine correctness risk: two processes could then believe they both hold the integration critical section (the original holder never checked its OWN lock is still valid before proceeding — it just runs to `finally { unlinkSync(lockPath) }` unconditionally, `factory/dev-loop.ts:2280-2288`, meaning the ORIGINAL holder's `unlinkSync` at the end would delete the SECOND holder's freshly-reclaimed lock file, and the original holder's in-process work (already past the stale check) continues believing it's still safe). | unit | GAP — critical: a slow-but-alive integration-lock holder can be double-entered past the 120s staleness window; the in-process mutex (`integrationMutexes`) only serializes within ONE Node process, so this is real for a KILLED-AND-RESPAWNED loop process racing the ORIGINAL loop process if somehow both are alive (e.g. supervisor relaunches the child while the old child is a lingering zombie that hasn't actually exited) — flag as critical. |
- [cross.chaos-process.31] | `skills-install.lock` DEAD pid vs. `skills-report.json` phase still "installing" (report not yet stale by its own 15-min clock). | `isSkillsLockLive` (pid-based) would correctly reclaim on a dead pid regardless of the report's own staleness clock — the pid check is authoritative and STRICTER (reclaims immediately on death, not after 15 minutes), so this direction (lock says dead, report says "recent") is SAFE; the dangerous direction is the reverse (scenario 13: lock says alive, report looks stale) which is NOT symmetric. | unit | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.32] | Progress-ledger lock (`progress-ledger.json.lock`) held by a DEAD pid vs. an ALIVE-but-slow writer (CAS retry storm under N=12 parallel, all racing to write near-simultaneously). | `reclaimStaleLock` (`factory/progress-ledger.ts:279-309`) uses the SAME `tooOld || ownerDead` OR-reclaim pattern as the integration lock (scenario 30) with a SHORTER stale window (30s vs 120s) — under N=12 parallel with heavy CAS contention (`CAS_MAX_ATTEMPTS=50`, `LOCK_RETRY_MS=25`ms retries), a genuinely busy-but-alive writer holding the lock for its write+CAS-retry-loop could in principle approach 30s if the ledger has grown large and 50 CAS attempts each re-read+re-write it, risking the same "stale reclaim on a still-alive slow holder" hazard as scenario 30, just with a tighter margin. | unit | GAP — same class of hazard as .30, flag together. |

### J. Two UI tabs / UI+CLI driving the same run

- [cross.chaos-process.33] | Tab A clicks "Run" (succeeds, becomes the lock holder); Tab B, unaware, also renders a "Run" button (its own status poll hasn't refreshed yet) and clicks it. | Tab B's POST hits `claimRunLock`'s EEXIST path, `isRunActive` sees Tab A's supervisor genuinely alive, throws `already_running` -> HTTP 409 (`app/api/control/start/route.ts:21`) — Tab B's UI must handle a 409 gracefully (show "already running", refresh status) rather than treating it as a generic error; this is a UI-state-handling requirement, not a data-corruption risk, since the lock layer already prevents any double-spawn. | e2e-ui | GAP — needs a UI-level test asserting the 409 renders as "already running" rather than a raw error toast. |
- [cross.chaos-process.34] | CLI (`vivicy stop --target <dir>` — the stop REQUIRES a target because the lock is per-project; CLI↔app parity, `lib/control.ts`) stops the run while the UI tab is mid-render of a "Stop" confirm dialog. | Once the CLI's `stopSupervisor` clears `run-state.json` and kills the process group, the UI's NEXT status poll sees `run_active:false`; if the user then confirms the STALE dialog's "Stop" button, the UI POSTs `/api/control/stop` again, hits `stopSupervisor`'s `if (!state) throw ControlError("not_running")` (`lib/control.ts:438-441`) -> HTTP 409 — again a clean, if user-surprising, honest failure requiring UI handling (dismiss dialog, show "already stopped") rather than a silent no-op. | e2e-ui | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.35] | UI (Extract button, gated per `control.spec.ts`'s "already extracted" aria-disabled test) and CLI simultaneously trigger `retry-stage {stage:"extract"}` while the OTHER already-running extraction from scenario 2 is in flight. | Since extraction has NO lock (confirmed absent across all of `lib/control.ts`), this compounds scenario 2: THREE concurrent extraction runs (two racing HTTP calls + one CLI) against the same target's canonical/baseline files, each independently freezing/authoring/writing `extraction-status.json` and racing each other's writes with no CAS or lock — the LAST writer of `extraction-status.json` wins arbitrarily, and the two "losing" runs' in-flight agent work (canonical edits, issue authoring) may partially interleave with the winner's, risking a corrupted or inconsistent issue corpus. | e2e-process | GAP — critical: the complete absence of an extraction lock is the single most consequential gap in this whole matrix; see critical GAPs summary. |

---

### K. Per-project runtime isolation + whole-target no-code enforcement

- [cross.chaos-process.36] | Two projects each hold an independent run-state lock: a supervised run in project A does not block a `start` in project B. | Both run locks coexist under distinct `projects/<key>/`; cross-project concurrent builds are legal, within-project single-run unchanged. | e2e-process | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.37] | Two projects' notification logs never collide. | `appendNotification` under A writes `projects/<keyA>/notifications.jsonl`; `readNotifications` for A never surfaces B's lines. | integration | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.38] | A mid-operation project switch DURING a skills install does not strand or clear the WRONG project's lock (`skillsLockPath` captured once via a threaded targetRoot). | The original project's `skills-install.lock` is the one released, regardless of a `getTargetRoot()` change between claim and release. | integration | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.39] | Legacy notifications/`vivi/` migration RACE: the app (which migrates) and the CLI (read-only), or two app requests, first-touch the same project namespace concurrently. | Best-effort migration; the CLI reader never migrates (reads legacy+project); no double-move corruption. | e2e-process | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.40] | Deleting ONE project's namespace `<runtime>/projects/<key>/` mid-run defeats only THAT project's locks; other projects' locks survive (blast radius now per-project, unlike a legacy whole-runtime-dir wipe). | Project B's `isRunActive`/notifications unaffected when A's namespace is removed. | e2e-process | GAP (refines cross.chaos-process.14) |
- [cross.chaos-process.41] | `projectRuntimeKey` stability across a process restart keeps a detached run's lock discoverable. | The recomputed key from the same absolute target path equals the pre-restart key; no orphaned lock under a new key. | integration | GAP |
- [cross.chaos-process.42] | TAMPER — self-hiding payload across cleanup passes: a leg rewrites `.gitignore` to ignore its own payload BEFORE writing it, so `git status` hides it; `cleanupCodeWritesDeep` restores `.gitignore` (unmasking) and the re-run `detectCodeWrites` removes the now-visible payload within the bounded passes. | `.gitignore` restored; payload removed; turn rejected. | e2e-process | vivi.test.ts ("a .gitignore self-hiding write is cleaned up in the second pass") |
- [cross.chaos-process.43] | TAMPER — payload survives the 3-pass bound: a cleanup that cannot converge within `pass < 3` leaves paths in `failed`, and the rejection is HONEST about it ("; WARNING: could not clean up: … — remove manually") rather than claiming a cleanup that did not happen (P1). | Still-dirty payload named in the loud warning. | e2e-process | GAP |
